result:
When night falls, I walk to the old train station to listen to the building breathe. The glass is fogged with the breath of years, and the clocks have all given up on time; one blinks 1:24 forever, a moth stuck in amber. The platform hums at a frequency you feel more than hear, like a memory trying to return through your feet.

A boy on a bike drifts past, wheels whispering to the rails. A vending machine glitters, all red promise, humming with potential in a place that has forgotten how to depart. I sit on the bench and read the graffiti that says nothing and everything, a conversation left behind by strangers. The station is a throat clearing itself, waiting.

A late commuter appears at the far end, a silhouette with a suitcase, scanning the empty horizon. He buys a paper from the machine, opens it like it’s a map, and pretends to read. He doesn’t board; he just stands, leaning into the night. The announcement speaker whispers once: Stand clear of the closing doors. I imagine it knows a secret we’ve all forgotten: journeys start before you step inside.

When the city sleeps, the station remembers. It is a lighthouse for the lonely, for the late, for the ones with stories to catch. We pass through, and something in us stays—stamped, timed, and finally set free.

I sit on the bench and read the graffiti that says nothing and everything, a conversation left behind by strangers. The station is a throat clearing itself, waiting.

A late commuter appears at the far end, a silhouette with a suitcase, scanning the empty horizon. He buys a paper from the machine, opens it like it’s a map, and pretends to read.
